message:
Harden sos_barrier arithmetic with saturating i128 ops

Replace raw `*`, `+`, `-` operations in the membrane runtime-math
SOS barrier evaluators with their `saturating_*` counterparts so that
pathological basis inputs can no longer cause silent i128 wrap or,
under debug builds, a panic in the allocator-hot path.

`evaluate_size_class_barrier_from_basis` previously built its 16-term
quadratic-form accumulator as a single chained `+` expression whose
intermediate `q[i][j] * bi * bj` products could, in principle, exceed
i128::MAX / 16 before the final `clamp` at the output. That is not a
realistic input today, but the SOS barrier is safety-critical and we
prefer saturation over wrap or panic. Rewrite as a mutable `acc: i128`
that ingests each term via `saturating_add(.saturating_mul(.)saturating_mul(.))`
so every intermediate pinned into the i128 range. The surrounding
`clamp(i64::MIN, i64::MAX)` cast is unchanged; the net effect is that
the score still lands in i64 but cannot be pushed outside that range
by a hostile basis.

`evaluate_provenance_barrier` gets the same treatment for the four
ppm-scaled terms it computes:

- `headroom`: `PROVENANCE_RISK_BUDGET_PPM - r` → `saturating_sub(r)`.
- `rb = r * b / one` and `rp = r * p / one`: the `*` become
  `saturating_mul`.
- `penalty_1`, `penalty_2`, `reward`: the `BETA_N * 1000` constants
  use `saturating_mul(1000)` (BETA_N is i64, so this prevents a
  pathological BETA from overflowing during the promotion), and the
  chained `rb * (one - v)` / `rp * (one - v)` / `v * (one - b)`
  products are rewritten as `.saturating_mul(.).saturating_mul(.)`
  with the `(one - ·)` complement factored into explicit
  `one_minus_v` / `one_minus_b` locals that come from
  `one.saturating_sub(·)`.
- `penalty_3 = BETA_4 * p / one`: `saturating_mul(p)`.
- The final `headroom - penalty_1 - penalty_2 - penalty_3 + reward`
  combinator becomes a chained `.saturating_sub(...).saturating_sub(...)
  .saturating_sub(...).saturating_add(reward)` so underflow on a
  hostile penalty stack saturates to `i128::MIN` rather than wrapping.

The final `.clamp(i64::MIN, i64::MAX) as i64` remains so callers see
the same i64 output range. Behavior is unchanged in the common case
where all inputs stay within the designed envelope; only edge cases
with extreme risk/bloom/arena-pressure signals diverge, and they now
diverge toward a safe clamped bound instead of a panic or wrap.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
